text: The surgeon prepares to place the laparoscopic ports using the baseball diamond concept, positioning a supraumbilical port for access to the surgical field.

The camera port is positioned approximately ten centimeters above the umbilicus due to the large size of the myoma. The surgeon places the right-hand port 7.5 centimeters laterally from the camera port.

The surgeon positions the left hand port 7.5 centimeters lateral to the supraumbilical camera port. A contralateral port is placed for the Miami screw, which will be used by both the surgeon's left hand and the assistant. The surgeon introduces a Veress needle using an ellipsoid number knife.

The surgeon creates a small two-millimeter incision at the supraumbilical point on the skin. A Veress needle is inserted perpendicular to the abdominal wall and oblique to the patient to establish pneumoperitoneum, followed by irrigation and suction tests.

The surgeon removes the Veress needle after the actual pressure reaches the preset level, with a flow rate of one liter per minute. The incision is enlarged to accommodate the primary trocar, which is introduced through the aluminum incision.

The surgeon uses a two-hand technique to insert the primary trocar for laparoscopic myomectomy. After establishing the primary port, diagnostic laparoscopy is performed. The second port is introduced 7.5 centimeters lateral and below the primary port, followed by the introduction of the third port, ensuring proper port positioning for the procedure.

The surgeon positions the right hand port 18 centimeters from the target umbilicus, with the telescope positioned 24 centimeters away. Two arcs are visible, measuring 18 and 24 centimeters respectively, indicating the spatial arrangement of the surgical instruments.
The surgeon introduces a 5 mm port for the right-hand dissector, harmonic, or suction irrigation tool. Another port is positioned 7.5 centimeters laterally, marking the last port placement.

The surgeon positions a port just above the anterior superior iliac spine, which will be used for the left hand myoma screw. A contralateral port is also positioned for assistance, and the initial diagnostic laparoscopy is performed.

The laparoscopic view reveals the uterus, which is entirely occupied by a myoma. Visible anatomical structures include the uterosacral ligament, the left fallopian tube, the round ligament, and the left infundibulopelvic (IP) ligament. The triangle of dome is also visible, with the ureter located below the IP ligament.
The surgeon visually identifies the ureter, noting its peristalsis, alongside the sacral pulmonary region. The sigmoid colon, cecum, and appendix are also visible in the surgical field.

The video shows the anatomical landmarks including the triangle of doom and triangle of pain. The round ligament is visible along with the deep ring, medial and lateral umbilical ligaments, and the inferior epigastric vessel on the right side. The pubic symphysis and median umbilical ligament are also identified. The left ovary and fimbria are observed, along with the IP ligament on the left side. The uterus is visible, occupied by the myoma.

The uterine artery is identified, and vasopressin is injected to induce transient ischemia. Approximately 200 ml of saline mixed with vasopressin is administered. The myoma screw is positioned to hold the uterus, and a horizontal elliptical incision is made on the uterine surface.
The surgeon prepares to make a horizontal incision on the uterus, aiming for an elliptical shape to facilitate easier removal of the myoma and subsequent suturing.

The surgeon uses a myoma screw in the left hand and suction irrigation in the right hand to gradually separate the myoma from the uterine muscle fibers. A small portion of serosa is sacrificed during the process. Another myoma screw is applied from the opposite side to assist in the separation, while muscle fibers on the right side are carefully cut to enlarge the separation area.

The surgeon uses a myoma screw to apply traction on the myoma, pushing it superiorly. Suction irrigation is employed to assist in separating the muscle layers. The myoma screw is repositioned to optimize traction, facilitating the gradual separation of the myoma from the surrounding tissue.

The surgeon demonstrates ambidextrous technique to achieve a good plane of dissection during laparoscopic myomectomy. The entire myoma is visible as it is being detached and extracted from the uterine base.

The surgeon completes the separation of the myoma, ensuring no tissue is left behind. The suture is prepared using a needle holder through a 5mm port, and intracorporeal four-layer suturing is performed to secure the surgical site.
The surgeon holds the serous layer that was removed, exposing the innermost muscle layer. The assistant holds the suture tail to facilitate continuous suturing of the internal muscle layer.

The surgeon performs suturing on the second layer of muscle tissue, ensuring no serosa is included. The suture progresses from right to left, and upon reaching the left corner, suction irrigation is used to clear blood from the area before returning the suture.

The surgeon is working on the muscle layer without involving the serosa, using the tail of the first suture layer to terminate the suturing process. A serger's knot or tumble square knot is used for termination, with the serger's knot being preferred.

The surgeon avoids holding the suture too tightly to prevent overshooting, then locks it in place. The zero muscular layer is sutured using a Dundee jamming knot technique, continuing the process of continuous suturing.
The surgeon uses suction to remove collected blood from the surgical area. A Dundee jamming knot is prepared, and suturing begins in the visible field.

The surgeon performs continuous suturing on the seromuscular layer, addressing a small tear caused by an electrical incision on the serosa. A starter knot is used, which self-locks when the needle is pulled through the loop. The fimbria are separated during the procedure.

The surgeon performs continuous suturing on the third layer, approximating the seromuscular layer with horizontal stitches. Irrigation and suction are used to clear blood from the surgical field, improving visibility as the suturing progresses towards the left corner.

The assistant holds the tissue as the surgeon applies the final suture to terminate the fourth layer, which involves approximating the serosa to serosa. The suture is pulled through the loop to create a self-locking knot.

The surgeon uses the left hand to hold the suture while the right hand continues suturing with the needle. The assistant holds the needle end of the third layer of the suture, which is not yet tied. This final layer reinforces the lower tier, with continuous suturing intermittently locking the series layer.

The surgeon reaches the left corner of the surgical site where there is slight oozing. A deep suture bite is taken to control the bleeding, resulting in the corner appearing paler due to ischemia. The needle is cut and removed to facilitate easier suturing. The third layer suture is terminated with the fourth layer suture, completing the secure four-layer closure.
The surgeon cuts the suture to finalize the closure, confirming that there is no bleeding in the surgical field. The ovary appears intact and unaffected.
The surgeon removes the suture, preparing to begin the process of morcellation. The morcellator is introduced into the surgical field to gradually fragment and extract the myoma tissue.

The surgeon completes the final stage of morselation, carefully extracting all morselated tissue fragments using a stone-holding forceps. A finger is inserted through the 15mm port to ensure complete removal. A cobbler's needle is used to pass and pull the suture through twice.

The surgeon highlights the 5mm port visible below, noting it is not necessary to close, while emphasizing the importance of closing the 15mm port to prevent hernia. The procedure performed was a laparoscopic myomectomy for pan neural myoma.
